region_of_origin: United States (Cajun tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general french fries entry; no specific Cajun-seasoned variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried potato products broadly; no Cajun-specific sub-code exists. Approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Cajun fries; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: Cajun spice blend (paprika, cayenne, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, salt)

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular serving of Cajun Fries is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a standard fast-food side portion of thick-cut skin-on fries.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated (FDC ID 170697) and USDA SR Legacy — Spices, paprika / cayenne blend adaptation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 300 kcal, protein 3.33 g, fat 16.67 g, carbohydrate 33.33 g, sodium 400 mg) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried seasoned potato fries (FDC ID 170697 and related entries). Values retained from input and cross-validated against USDA. Cajun seasoning contribution to sodium is consistent with the 400 mg/100 g figure. Micronutrients (calcium 13.33 mg, iron 1.0 mg, vitamin C 6.67 mg) are plausible for skin-on potato fries and consistent with USDA potato fry data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water set to -1 where not directly reported in the input; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; French fries / fried potato analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Cajun-seasoned skin-on fries. The GI of 75 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) for deep-fried potato french fries, which consistently report GI in the range 63–87 (mean ~75, glucose reference). Cajun seasoning does not materially alter the GI of the potato base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 × 33.33 × 1.5) / 100 = 37.5 g per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g from Cajun seasoning salt; exceeds the borderline threshold for hypertension-sensitive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Cajun Fries due to 400 mg sodium per 100 g (600 mg per regular serving), which represents approximately 26% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit in a single side dish; request reduced-salt preparation and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and 33.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g; deep-frying elevates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Cajun Fries because the high GI (~75) combined with 50 g available carbohydrate per regular serving produces a glycemic load of approximately 37.5, causing a significant postprandial blood glucose spike; if consumed, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables and limit to a small portion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16.67 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.67 g/100 g), trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), and sodium (400 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and Cajun seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of Cajun Fries due to the combination of high total fat from deep-frying, trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.67 g/100 g), and elevated sodium (400 mg/100 g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; occasional small portions are preferable to regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; potato and Cajun spices (paprika, cayenne) are nightshade-derived and may worsen symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Cajun Fries because deep-frying generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation, and the nightshade ingredients (potato, paprika, cayenne) may trigger solanine-related inflammatory responses in susceptible individuals; baked or air-fried potato alternatives are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cajun Fries (Regular) are thick-cut, skin-on potato fries seasoned with a Cajun spice blend — typically comprising paprika, cayenne pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, dried oregano, and salt — then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The skin-on preparation retains more dietary fibre (3.33 g/100 g) and micronutrients compared with peeled fries. The Cajun seasoning imparts a smoky, mildly spicy, and savoury flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 300 kcal, 3.33 g protein, 16.67 g fat (saturated fat 2.67 g, trans fat 0.13 g), 33.33 g carbohydrate, 0.67 g sugar, and 400 mg sodium. A regular serving is approximately 150 g (450 kcal, 600 mg sodium). The glycemic index is high (~75), making this food unsuitable for blood glucose management. The elevated sodium from Cajun seasoning raises concern for hypertension. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-compatible. It is commonly served as a side dish or snack in Western fast-food and casual dining contexts in Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Cajun fries?
Is Cajun fries high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of Cajun fries?
How much fat is in Cajun fries?
Can I eat Cajun fries if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in Cajun fries?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried seasoned potato fries and have been cross-validated. No exact Cajun fries entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central; values are adapted from the closest USDA fried potato entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato fries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Cajun-seasoned deep-fried skin-on fries is not directly reported in the input data; USDA FoodData Central reports MUFA ~7.5 g and PUFA ~5.5 g per 100 g for generic deep-fried potato fries (FDC ID 170697), but these values have not been adopted without exact match confirmation and are therefore left as -1 pending direct source verification. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in input; USDA data suggests ~400–430 mg/100 g for skin-on potato fries but not confirmed for this specific Cajun preparation. water_g set to -1: not reported in input or confirmed from an approved source for this specific preparation. All other vitamin and mineral fields set to -1 are not reported in the input and are not confirmed from an approved source for this specific Cajun fries preparation. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero for a plant-based deep-fried potato product with no animal-derived ingredients; this is consistent with USDA data for vegetable-oil-fried potato fries. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, AJCN) data for deep-fried potato french fries; no Cajun-specific GI study was identified. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database and has been replaced with the USDA FoodData Central as the primary approved nutrient source. 5. Ethnic tags in the input ('Wings', 'Fried Chicken') appear to be erroneous carry-overs from another record and have been excluded from the output. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for Cajun-seasoned skin-on fries exists.
